ccel.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

title: Concerning Energy.

source.title: NPNF2-09. Hilary of Poitiers, John of Damascus

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: All the faculties we have already discussed, both those of knowledge and those of life, both the natural and the artificial, are, it is to be noted, called energies.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For energy is the natural force and activity of each essence: or again, natural energy is the activity innate in every essence: and so, clearly, things that have the same essence have also the same energy, and things that have different natures have also different energies.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For no essence can be devoid of natural energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Natural energy again is the force in each essence by which its nature is made manifest.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And again: natural energy is the primal, eternally-moving force of the intelligent soul: that is, the eternally-moving word of the soul, which ever springs naturally from it.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And yet again: natural energy is the force and activity of each essence which only that which is not lacks.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: But actions are also called energies: for instance, speaking, eating, drinking, and such like.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The natural affections also are often called energies, for instance, hunger, thirst, and so forth.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: And yet again, the result of the force is also often called energy.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Things are spoken of in a twofold way as being potential and actual.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: For we say that the child at the breast is a potential scholar, for he is so equipped that, if taught, he will become a scholar.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Further, we speak of a potential and an actual scholar, meaning that the latter is versed in letters, while the former has the power of interpreting letters, but does not put it into actual use: again, when we speak of an actual scholar, we mean that he puts his power into actual use, that is to say, that he really interprets writings.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: It is, therefore, to be observed that in the second sense potentiality and actuality go together; for the scholar is in the one case potential, and in the other actual.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: The primal and only true energy of nature is the voluntary or rational and independent life which constitutes our humanity.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: I know not how those who rob the Lord of this can say that He became man.

verses.book: An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith.
verses.text: Energy is drastic activity of nature: and by drastic is meant that which is moved of itself.
